dynamic programming

dynamic programming amounts to breaking down an optimization problem into simpler sub-problems, and storing the solution to each sub-problem so that each sub-problem is only solved once. to be honest, this definition may not make total sense until you see an example of a sub-problem

provide an algorithm that is as efficient as possible that solves the following problem:
input: an array of natural numbers
output: a list of k pairs () of indicies of the form i,i+1 such that none of the indicies of the array appears more than once and the sum is maximal
example: for the input 12,11,2,4,1,10,8,12 the output should be (11,2),(4,1),(10,8)

show the correctness of the algorithm you proposed

we prove the correctness of the algorithm using induction on the size of the input array

  1. base step: for , the solution, , would equal , and C to a singleton of which is the only pair in the array so it is the solution
  2. the induction hypothesis: we assume truth for
  3. the induction step, we show truth for
    on the last iteration of the for loop , we would be checking which of the values , is bigger, the first corresponding to a combination of pairs whose sum would’ve been maximal up until , and the second corresponding to a combination of pairs whose sum would’ve been maximal up until , of those two, we pick one and add to it yet another pair only if the addition of that pair makes the sum stay or become bigger than the sum of the other combination with the other new pair added to it, so the last sum in B would still be maximal, and the pairs in C, likewise, would be corresponding to the newly found maximal sum

given an array of numbers of size , find the maximal sum of any elements in the array, under the constraints that from each row only one element is picked, and each element has to be picked in the same column as the one before, or the column after or the column before

write pseudocode that solves the problem

show the correctness of the algorithm

find the time complexity of the algorithm


given the following problem:
input: an array of positive numbers of size
output: a subarray (non-consecutive) whose sum is maximal that doesnt contain two consecutive elements
example: input output 12
example: input output 14
example: input output 22
example: input output 29

we can stem the following 2 lemmas:

the optimal solution (i.e. the subarray whose sum is maximal) contains atleast a single element of every three consecutive elements

we assume in contradiction that for the index i, such that , the optimal solution doesnt contain A[i],A[i+1], and A[i+2], given that A[i+1]>0, appending A[i+1] to the given solution would give us an array with a bigger sum than the array we already have which results in a contradiction

the optimal solution always contains one of A[n],A[n-1], but obviously not both

if A[n-2] is not included in a given solution, then max(A[n-1],A[n]) is included, if A[n-2] is included in a solution, its obvious that a[n] is also included

every optimal subarray (which there are a few) contains A[n-1] or A[n], if we find a subarray that contains A[n] and has the biggest sum of the arrays that contain A[n] and find a subarray that contains A[n-1] that has the biggest sum of those that contain A[n-1] then the one with the bigger sum of both is the solution
to find them, we define the recursive function P1R such that the call P1R(A,i) would find the solution for P1 for the subarray under the assumption that A[i] is included in the solution
for the array A=[3,12,4,6] we’d have P1R(A,3) = A[1]+A[3] = 7
to understand how the function P1R works, we notice that given A[i] is part of the output so A[i-1] cant be included, and A[i-3] and A[i-2] cant both be included so:

so we finally write the algorithm:

this solution works, but its time complexity is probably exponential
the arguments that gets called with are the numbers , a total of numbers, so the function gets called with the same arguments many times, to get over this and achieve a linear time complexity we use some sort of “cache”, a look up table, which is what dynamic programming essentially proposes
the cache we propose is an array , of size , such that is defined as the return value of , the algorithm finds without calling

given a square maze of the size , we start in the upper left corner and have to arrive at the lower right corner by going down/right but not through a wall, in how many ways can this be done?

we use an additional array B, such that B[i] is the number of ways to arrive from the point (i,j) in the maze to the end

given an array of real numbers, propose an algorithm that finds the maximal sum of a subarray that doesnt contain 3 consecutive elements

we use two additional arrays and , such that is the solution to the problem in the subarray such that the element is part of the sum, and is the solution to the problem in the subarray without being part of the sum

an algorithm that finds how many permutations there are of the set 0,1,2 that dont contain consecutive 1’s or 2’s (consecutive 0’s are allowed)
we use 3 additional arrays

  1. := the number of permutations of length i in which 0 is the last number
  2. := the number of permutations of length i in which 1 is the last number
  3. := the number of permutations of length i in which 2 is the last number

matrix multiplication using dynamic programming

refer to matrix multiplication for the mathematics
given the matrices , the number of scalar multiplication operations needed to compute is , the number of addition operations needed to compute is which is close but a little smaller

given three multipliable matrices , we want to find the number of multiplication operations required to compute
there are a few “timings” (order of application) in which we can apply the multiplication:

  1. the timing (1,2) which denotes the order of multiplication , which takes operations
  2. the timing (2,1) which denotes the order which takes operations

we denote for
the number of the operation between and would be
every permutation of the sequence corresponds to a particular timing each with a different cost but the same resulting matrix
given the multiplication of matrices , we define the cost of timing , as the number of multiplication operations needed to compute according to
the cost of finding is defined as the minimal cost of a given timing

we want an algorithm that finds the best timing (one with the minimal cost). a straightforward algorithm goes through each timing and finds the one with the minimal cost which would take time because a timing is a permutation of a sequence of numbers
we try a recursive solution. for each , we denote by the number of minimal multiplications required to find . with this notation, the cost of computing is
the algorithm is based on the following 2 lemmas:



if we were to follow the steps of the algorithm, we’d notice the following:
to find mc(1,5) the algorithm has to find the minimum of:

and to find mc(2,5) we’d need to find the minimum of:

we’d notice for example that mc(3,5) and mc(4,5) are being calculated twice, so the algorithm does some repeated work that can be reduced using dynamic programming
the algorithm has a lower bound of and while it is better than the straightforward solution, it is still inefficient
each of the two arguments that the function mc takes can vary between n values, so the total number of permutations of arguments it can take is , so we use a lookup table of size and use it to store the results of executing the function mc with each possible configuration of the arguments
